channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Colin Allred, Former Lawmaker, Wins Democratic Runoff for House Seat
item.description: Mr. Allred beat the incumbent, Representative Julie Johnson, and is now favored to win the general election in a heavily Democratic Dallas-based district.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar/Getty Images
item.media:description: Colin Allred served three terms in the House of Representatives before giving up his seat to run unsuccessfully for the Senate in 2024.

item.title: Cornyn Crushed: 7 Takeaways From Tuesday’s Runoffs in Texas
item.description: President Trump’s record of ousting those he sees as disloyal continued apace with Senator John Cornyn’s defeat. Whether his relationship with Senate Republicans can be repaired is another question.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton, the Republican attorney general of Texas, defeated Senator John Cornyn in a runoff Tuesday.

item.title: Trump-Backed Candidate Wins G.O.P. Race in San Antonio District
item.description: Carlos De La Cruz, a first-time candidate, defeated a moderate rival in a U.S. House district that was redrawn to favor Republicans, but where Democrats see an opportunity.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Michael Gonzalez/Associated Press
item.media:description: Carlos De La Cruz, a Republican running in Texas’ 35th Congressional District, speaking in February before an appearance by President Trump in Corpus Christi, Texas.

item.title: Johnny Garcia Beats Texas Democrat Accused of Being Antisemitic in House Runoff
item.description: The victory by Johnny Garcia, a sheriff’s deputy, brought relief to Democrats, who hope to hold onto a seat that Republicans redrew to flip it.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Callaghan O'Hare for The New York Times
item.media:description: Johnny Garcia, a sheriff’s deputy, was the preferred candidate of Democratic leaders in Washington.

item.title: Mayes Middleton Defeats Chip Roy in the Runoff for Texas Attorney General
item.description: After connecting his opponent to past criticism of President Trump, the conservative state senator now advances to the general election.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Shafkat Anowar/The Dallas Morning News, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Mayes Middleton in March at the Conservative Political Action Conference in Grapevine, Texas.

item.title: Ken Paxton Ousts John Cornyn, Solidifying Trump’s Grip on Republican Party Voters
item.description: Ken Paxton, the Texas attorney general, overcame scandals and a significant fund-raising disadvantage to win. His victory sets up the general-election clash that Democrats had hoped for.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy, J. David Goodman and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton, the attorney general of Texas, at his watch party in March in Plano. Mr. Paxton went on to defeat Senator John Cornyn in the Republican primary runoff election.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Ken Paxton, the Republican Senate Nominee in Texas
item.description: With the support of President Trump, Mr. Paxton unseated Senator John Cornyn in a high-profile Republican runoff.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton, the Texas attorney general, received an endorsement last week from President Trump.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About John Cornyn, Republican Senate Hopeful in Texas
item.description: He is fighting for political survival against a primary challenger backed by President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Cornyn, Republican of Texas, narrowly led in the first round of primary voting in March.

item.title: Christian Menefee Defeats Al Green in Texas Democratic House Runoff
item.description: The showdown, manufactured by a Republican gerrymander, created one of many generational clashes consuming Democrats this cycle.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Christian Menefee became the first Democrat to defeat an incumbent this year, felling 11-term Representative Al Green.

item.title: As Republicans Battled in the Runoff, Talarico Sought to Unify Democrats
item.description: James Talarico, the Democratic nominee for U.S. Senate, has been raising money and looking to unite his party for the general election.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: State Representative James Talarico of Texas at a rally in Austin after the primary election in March.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Al Green, Democratic House Candidate in Texas
item.description: He is a veteran lawmaker and a vocal critic of President Trump who is facing a runoff against a fellow Democratic member espousing generational change.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Al Green, Democrat of Texas, during a committee hearing in February.

item.title: 5 Things to Know About Christian Menefee, Democratic House Nominee in Texas
item.description: Mr. Menefee, one of the newest members of Congress, beat Representative Al Green, a Democrat who is more than twice his age, in a primary runoff.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Annie Mulligan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Christian D. Menefee during a campaign event in March.

item.title: Trump May Appear at N.B.A. Finals in New York
item.description: President Trump is said to be considering the appearance after his hometown team, the Knicks, clinched a championship spot.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: David Richard/Imagn Images, via Reuters Connect
item.media:description: Members of the New York Knicks celebrating as Eastern Conference champions on Monday in Cleveland.

item.title: When to Expect Results in the Texas Primary Runoff
item.description: Unofficial results will start coming in after polls close at 7 p.m. local time. They will be posted on the state’s voter portal.
item.dc:creator: Taylor Robinson
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Texas voters are casting ballots in a variety of primary runoffs on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Administration Wants Employees to Sign NDAs
item.description: Lawyers representing federal workers said the move is intended to chill speech and could be challenged on First Amendment grounds.
item.dc:creator: Eileen Sullivan
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Office of Personnel Management drafted a proposal that federal employees sign a nondisclosure agreement.

item.title: Runoff Polls in Texas Senate Contest Have Favored Ken Paxton
item.description: Mr. Paxton, a Texas Senate candidate, has led Senator John Cornyn in all but one independent poll in the Republican primary runoff.
item.dc:creator: Caroline Soler
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton, the Texas state attorney general, received a bump in two polls after being endorsed by President Trump.

item.title: Trump Administration to Send Americans Exposed to Ebola to Kenya
item.description: In past outbreaks, Americans exposed to the virus were sent home to be treated in state-of-the-art facilities. The Trump administration has already flown some U.S. citizens to Europe for treatment.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.media:credit: Abubaker Lubowa/Reuters
item.media:description: A poster outside a medical center in Kampala, Uganda, warns of an Ebola outbreak.

item.title: The Factors Shaping the 2026 Midterm Elections
item.description: President Trump has sought revenge in G.O.P. primaries. Democrats have turned out to vote in large numbers. Republicans have gained a redistricting advantage.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: Poll workers in Cypress, Texas, on Tuesday.

item.title: Democratic Incumbents Run Against Each Other After Republican Redistricting in Texas
item.description: A Republican-led redistricting effort forced Representative Al Green, who has served 11 terms, and Christian Menefee, one of the newest members of Congress, into a battle for the same seat.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Radcliffe
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: Campaign signs for Al Green and Christian Menefee in a Houston neighborhood in the 18th congressional district.

item.title: Angela Paxton endorses three candidates in runoffs, but not her estranged husband.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher and Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: State Senator Angela Paxton in her office in Austin, Texas, last year.

item.title: Southern Poverty Law Center Accuses Justice Dept. of Vindictive Prosecution
item.description: Defense lawyers are increasingly accusing the Justice Department under President Trump of using its powers to prioritize the president’s political imperatives above the pursuit of actual justice.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Jada Yuan/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Southern Poverty Law Center’s headquarters in Montgomery, Ala. Formed in 1971, the center is best known for investigating the Ku Klux Klan and other white supremacy organizations.

item.title: The Jaw-Dropping Cost of the Texas Senate Primary: Nearly $130 Million
item.description: The race between Senator John Cornyn and his right-wing rival, Ken Paxton, has cost mountains of cash. More of it has gone to help Mr. Cornyn — but that money may not be enough.
item.dc:creator: Matt Zdun and Theodore Schleifer
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Republican primary race for Senate between Senator John Cornyn and Ken Paxton, the Texas attorney general, has become the most expensive primary campaign in the country’s recent history.

item.title: Iran Threatens to Renew Strikes Against U.S. Amid Push for Diplomatic Deal
item.description: The warnings came after U.S. forces struck military sites in Iran. Secretary of State Marco Rubio said on Tuesday that negotiations to end the war were continuing.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak, Erika Solomon, Euan Ward and Michael Levenson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A crowd carried photos of Iran’s three supreme leaders during a ceremony in Tehran on Sunday.

item.title: Trump embraced an ally over an incumbent in the Texas Senate race.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton, the Texas attorney general, has maneuvered for President Trump’s support ahead of the May 26 runoff election in the Republican primary for Senate in the state.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Lawsuit Over Commercial Driver’s Licenses for Migrants
item.description: Florida officials had asked the justices to hear a lawsuit accusing California and Washington of improperly granting licenses to an immigrant accused in a fatal crash.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Benjamin Fanjoy,/Associated Press
item.media:description: Harjinder Singh being escorted by Lt. Gov. Jay Collins of Florida and members of law enforcement in Stockton, Calif., in August. Mr. Singh is accused of causing a fatal crash in Florida by making an illegal U-turn.

item.title: Trump’s snub of Cornyn comes with a cost.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump endorsed Senator John Cornyn’s primary opponent in the Texas Senate race.

item.title: Texas’ Redistricting Creates Intraparty Clashes Over House Seats
item.description: The new congressional map Texas Republicans drew to favor the party has led to several contentious primary runoffs.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: Democratic and Republican lawmakers debating the redistricting bill at the State Capitol in Austin in August.

item.title: Supreme Court Reverses Ruling in Immigration Judges’ Free Speech Lawsuit
item.description: A group of immigration judges in 2020 challenged work-related restrictions on their public speaking engagements, saying they violated their free speech rights.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: The justices of the Supreme Court said the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit had overstepped in its ruling. There were no noted dissents.

item.title: The G.O.P. Race for Texas Attorney General Grew Heated, and Expensive
item.description: In the last weeks of the contest, Representative Chip Roy reported raising more than $8 million, and he intensified his attacks on Mayes Middleton, a state senator.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McGaughy

item.title: UFC Unveils Plans for White House Fight on Trump’s Birthday
item.description: The Ultimate Fighting Championship bout is scheduled for June 14, President Trump’s 80th birthday.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction on the South Lawn of the White House on Monday, ahead of events for the United States’ 250th anniversary that include the upcoming UFC fight.

item.title: Ken Paxton has a history of winning primaries and make-or-break runoffs.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kruse and Lauren McGaughy
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ken Paxton, the Texas attorney general, at a campaign stop in Victoria, Texas, last month.

item.title: Trump Declares Himself in Good Health After Physical Exam at Walter Reed
item.description: President Trump, who will turn 80 next month, did not give details on the exam.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.category: Reed, Walter, National Military Medical Center
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has been observed with swelling in his legs, which the president’s doctors and aides said was a result of chronic venous insufficiency.

item.title: Court Rejects Alabama House Map, Calling It Unfair to Black Voters
item.description: Alabama is likely to appeal the ruling, which stops an effort to use a new congressional map that would likely cost Democrats a majority-Black district.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane and Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Wes Frazer for The New York Times
item.media:description: People march for voting rights on the famed Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma, Ala., in May.

item.title: Judge to Decide How Long Torture’s Taint Infected the Sept. 11 Case
item.description: Eight days of legal arguments at Guantánamo have brought the long-running terrorism case to a moment of truth in the long wait for justice.
item.dc:creator: Carol Rosenberg
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: A detainee at the military prison at Guantánamo Bay in 2019. The case of the Sept. 11, 2001, terrorist attacks is in its 15th year of pretrial proceedings, with no date set for the trial to begin.

item.title: Rahm Emanuel, a Possible 2028 Contender, Calls for Higher Education Reforms
item.description: Republicans have made gains with voters on education issues. A plan championed by Mr. Emanuel, a Democratic former mayor of Chicago, centers on college affordability.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rahm Emanuel, the former mayor of Chicago, calls his plan a “grand bargain” between universities, the federal government and American families.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Christopher Lee for The New York Times

item.title: As Trump Politicizes Justice Dept., Prosecutors Struggle With Grand Juries
item.description: Judges and grand juries have increasingly lost faith in the Justice Department as the president uses it to reward his friends and go after his opponents.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The examples of grand jury malfeasance come on top of the many cases in which Justice Department prosecutors have failed to get grand jurors to return indictments.

item.title: What to Watch in Texas Runoff Elections: Trump Endorsements, Democratic Incumbents and More
item.description: All eyes will be on the Republican Senate runoff between the incumbent John Cornyn and Attorney General Ken Paxton. But Tuesday’s runoffs in Texas will feature other important contests.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times; Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times

item.title: In the Georgia Governor’s Race, an Election Denier is the G.O.P. Front-Runner
item.description: Burt Jones, the leading G.O.P. candidate in Georgia, worked with Trump allies to try to overturn the election, and even talked to the president himself.
item.dc:creator: Danny Hakim and Richard Fausset
item.category: Georgia Criminal Case Against Trump (2020 Election)
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: While campaigning, Burt Jones boasts about his efforts in 2020.
